angle: Off-corpus false-relevance in the score calibration: why an unrelated doc earns a calibrated 0.78 "good" and how to negative-calibrate / corpus-ground the verdict
Off-corpus false-relevance in the score calibration: why an unrelated doc earns a calibrated 0.78 "good" and how to negative-calibrate / corpus-ground the verdict
title: Add a corpus-grounding (off-corpus) gate to the request-quality verdict, not just the per-result band
problem: The 'good' verdict is produced purely from absolute cosine relevance with no corpus-grounding signal. In confidence-scoring.ts:453 `topScore = resolveCalibrationScore(results[0])`, which routes through resolveCalibrationScore (lines 170-174) -> resolveAbsoluteRelevance (pipeline/types.ts:90-97), returning the raw normalized cosine of the top hit. The 'good' rules at confidence-scoring.ts:471 (`topScore >= 0.8`) and :476 (`topScore >= 0.7 && (qualityRatio >= 0.6 || topMargin >= 0.15)`) therefore fire on any sufficiently high cosine, regardless of whether the query term exists in the corpus. The benchmark (029 benchmark-results.md:71,81) confirms the off-corpus term 'kubernetes' scored 0.78 cosine on the unrelated 'Spec Memory Runtime Retention Cleanup' doc and was waved through as 'good' identically across all four models, while in-corpus 'authentication' correctly stayed weak at 0.50. Nomic-embed-text-v1.5 (referenced evidence-gap-detector.ts:19) returns a high non-zero cosine floor for semantically generic-but-unrelated text, and nothing in the verdict path discounts that floor.
evidence: confidence-scoring.ts:453,471,476 (verdict reads raw cosine); pipeline/types.ts:90-97 (resolveAbsoluteRelevance returns raw normalized cosine, no grounding); 029 benchmark-results.md:81 ('a property of the absolute-relevance calibration and not a model behavior'); evidence-gap-detector.ts:19 (nomic-embed-text-v1.5 is the active embedder).
proposal: Introduce a corpus-grounding signal consumed by assessRequestQuality before it can return 'good'. Concretely: (a) compute a lexical/term-overlap grounding score between the query tokens and the top hit's indexed content/anchors/title (the corpus already stores BM25 token data via bm25-index), and (b) require BOTH the cosine topScore AND a minimum grounding score to award 'good'; when cosine is high but grounding is near-zero (the off-corpus signature), downgrade to 'weak'. Gate the whole behavior behind a new default-off flag (mirror SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION in search-flags.ts) so it ships dark and graduates after eval. This keeps the deterministic envelope contract intact (good/weak/gap still emitted) while closing the lexically-ungrounded high-cosine hole.
benefit: Off-corpus terms like 'kubernetes' stop earning 'good' and a spurious cite, while genuine high-cosine in-corpus hits (which also carry lexical grounding) are unaffected. Directly closes the single false-positive the 144-cell benchmark surfaced.
risk: A naive lexical-overlap floor could wrongly downgrade legitimate paraphrase/synonym queries that have low literal token overlap (semantic match, no lexical match). Mitigation: tune the grounding floor low and only apply the downgrade when cosine is high AND grounding is essentially zero; validate against the existing ground-truth set before graduating the flag.

angle: Off-corpus false-relevance in the score calibration: why an unrelated doc earns a calibrated 0.78 "good" and how to negative-calibrate / corpus-ground the verdict
title: Add a null-query / background-cosine baseline so absolute relevance is measured above the corpus floor, not above zero
problem: resolveAbsoluteRelevance (pipeline/types.ts:90-97) treats raw normalized cosine as an absolute 0-1 relevance, implicitly assuming an unrelated doc scores ~0. With nomic embeddings that assumption is false: unrelated text sits at a high background cosine, so 0.78 for 'kubernetes' is mostly the corpus's background-similarity floor, not real relevance. The thresholds in confidence-scoring.ts (HIGH_THRESHOLD=0.7 at :39, TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD=0.8 at :78) are calibrated against a 0-1 scale whose effective zero is well above 0, so the same numeric 0.78 means 'strong' for one embedder and 'background noise' for another. There is no background/null-query baseline anywhere in the path: the only similarity floor is MULTI_CONCEPT_MIN_SIMILARITY=0.5 (stage1-candidate-gen.ts:76), which applies ONLY to multi-concept search, never to the bare hybrid /memory:search path the benchmark used.
evidence: pipeline/types.ts:90-97 (raw cosine treated as absolute, zero-baseline assumed); confidence-scoring.ts:39,78 (0.7/0.8 thresholds on the raw 0-1 scale); stage1-candidate-gen.ts:76 (the only min-similarity floor is 0.5 and multi-concept-only); evidence-gap-detector.ts:17-22 (embedder changed jina-v3 -> nomic, confirming the scale is embedder-dependent and re-tuned over time).
proposal: Estimate a per-embedder background cosine floor (e.g. mean top-1 cosine of a set of known-off-corpus probe queries, or a periodic offline calibration emitting a `backgroundCosine` constant) and rescale absolute relevance as `(cosine - backgroundFloor) / (1 - backgroundFloor)` clamped to [0,1] before it feeds the 0.7/0.8 verdict thresholds. This makes the thresholds embedder-portable and pushes off-corpus terms below 'good' by construction, because their raw cosine is at-or-near the background floor. Persist the floor alongside the calibration model under lib/eval/data and gate via a flag.
benefit: Makes absolute-relevance thresholds robust to the embedder's background-similarity floor, so off-corpus false-relevance is suppressed at the source and the same thresholds survive future embedder swaps without silent recalibration.
risk: Estimating a stable background floor requires a representative off-corpus probe set; too-aggressive a floor compresses the dynamic range and could push borderline-relevant in-corpus hits below 'good'. Mitigation: derive the floor empirically from off-corpus probes, re-fit when the embedder changes, and ship behind a default-off flag with eval validation.

angle: Off-corpus false-relevance in the score calibration: why an unrelated doc earns a calibrated 0.78 "good" and how to negative-calibrate / corpus-ground the verdict
title: Add off-corpus hard-negative queries to the eval ground-truth so the gate-verdict harness actually catches this false-positive
problem: The infrastructure to catch this already exists but is never exercised on the off-corpus case. computeGateVerdictMetrics (eval-metrics.ts:770-797) computes TP/FP/TN/FN and treats a 'good' verdict on a non-citable query as a false positive, and the label view sets `expectedCitable = !isHardNegative && maxRelevance >= threshold` (eval-metrics.ts ~:747-760), so a hard-negative query is correctly expected to be non-citable. BUT all 6 hard_negative ground-truth queries (ground-truth.json:910-975) are in-corpus near-miss decoys ('the BM25 indexing fidelity remediation lane...', 'the causal reducer that derives session-trace edges...', each with a specific target memoryId and an EXCLUDED sibling decoy). None is an off-corpus/off-topic term like 'kubernetes' that should surface nothing citable at all. The exact failure mode the benchmark found is therefore invisible to the eval suite, which is why a 0.78-good-on-unrelated-doc regression ships green.
evidence: eval-metrics.ts:770-797 (computeGateVerdictMetrics already classifies good-on-non-citable as FP); eval-metrics.ts label view `expectedCitable = !isHardNegative && maxRelevance >= threshold`; ground-truth.json:910-975 (all 6 existing hard-negatives are in-corpus near-miss decoys, confirmed by their expectedResultDescription 'Decoy EXCLUDED: ...'); 029 benchmark-results.md:81 (the off-corpus FP is real and model-invariant).
proposal: Add a small set of off-corpus hard_negative queries to ground-truth.json (e.g. 'kubernetes', 'oauth token refresh', and 2-3 other terms with zero genuine corpus targets), each with an empty relevance set so expectedCitable resolves to false. Then surface the off-corpus FP rate as an explicit metric/assertion in the gate-verdict harness, so any verdict regression on off-corpus terms fails the eval. This is the cheapest change and it also guards whichever calibration fix (proposal 1 or 2) lands.
benefit: Turns the benchmark's one-off finding into a permanent, cheap regression guard: any future change that lets an off-corpus term earn 'good' fails the eval. Also provides the labeled off-corpus probes needed to fit the background floor in proposal 2 and validate the grounding gate in proposal 1.
risk: Very low. Adding queries with empty relevance sets is additive to the eval corpus; only risk is choosing 'off-corpus' terms that later become in-corpus as the spec tree grows, producing a stale false-negative label. Mitigation: pick terms structurally unrelated to spec-kit (infra/web/ML jargon) and document them as off-corpus probes.

angle: Off-corpus false-relevance in the score calibration: why an unrelated doc earns a calibrated 0.78 "good" and how to negative-calibrate / corpus-ground the verdict
title: Re-fit (or re-couple) the isotonic calibration curve so the calibrated value reflects off-corpus non-relevance, and note that calibration alone cannot fix the verdict
problem: The shipped isotonic calibration model cannot move the verdict and is itself fitted on a corpus-derived proxy, so it actively masks the issue. Two confirmed facts: (1) The band/verdict is derived from the PRE-calibration rebalanced value, NOT the calibrated probability (confidence-scoring.ts:400 `const label = toConfidenceLabel(rebalancedValue)` with the comment at :390-399 explaining the calibrated value is deliberately bypassed for banding), so applyCalibration (confidence-calibration.ts:254-273) changes only the numeric `confidence.value`, never good/weak/gap. (2) The committed model (lib/eval/data/confidence-calibration-model.json) tops out at y=0.2 for x=0.758 and is fitted on 879 samples that confidence-calibration.ts:8-14 explicitly flags as 'a CORPUS-DERIVED PROXY (spec titles/keywords as queries, the spec's own memory as the positive), NOT human-judged live traffic' and 'UNVALIDATED INFRASTRUCTURE'. A proxy that only ever pairs a query with its own spec's memory as the positive has no off-corpus negatives in it, so it learns nothing about off-corpus false-relevance.
evidence: confidence-scoring.ts:388,400,390-399 (verdict bands off rebalancedValue, calibrated value deliberately bypassed); confidence-calibration.ts:8-18 ('UNVALIDATED INFRASTRUCTURE', 'CORPUS-DERIVED PROXY', off by default until live labels collected); confidence-calibration-model.json (fittedFrom 879, max y=0.2, fittedUnderAbsoluteRelevance true); confidence-scoring.ts:239-254 (coupling guard already exists).
proposal: Two parts. First, document explicitly (in the module and in 005's research) that the calibration curve is a per-result probability cosmetic and CANNOT close the verdict false-positive — the fix must live in assessRequestQuality (proposals 1/2), not in the curve. Second, when the labeled-traffic follow-up runs, include the off-corpus hard-negatives from proposal 3 as true negatives in the fitting set so the refitted curve assigns genuinely low P(relevant) to high-cosine-off-corpus inputs; this at least makes the surfaced numeric confidence honest even though the verdict is fixed elsewhere. Keep both behind the existing SPECKIT_CONFIDENCE_CALIBRATION flag and the fittedUnderAbsoluteRelevance coupling guard (confidence-scoring.ts:239-254).
benefit: Prevents wasted effort re-tuning the curve to fix a verdict it provably cannot move, and makes the surfaced confidence number honest about off-corpus hits once real labels (including off-corpus negatives) are collected.
risk: Largely a no-op for the immediate false-positive (by design — that is the point of the proposal). Real risk is opportunity cost: treating curve-refitting as the fix would burn the L-effort labeled-traffic collection without closing the verdict hole. Lower priority precisely because the verdict fix (proposals 1-3) is where the leverage is.
